skill.name: git-submodule
skill.description: Guides setup, update, removal, and troubleshooting workflows for Git submodules in multi-repository projects.
skill.summary: Manage Git submodules for external dependencies and shared repository components.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis mostly matched Markdown code fences, Git command examples, hardcoded sample URLs, and expected .gitmodules access. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found, but the skill can cause agents to run networked and destructive Git commands if used without confirmation. Publish with a warning that users should review repository URLs, paths, and cleanup commands before execution.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Git Cleanup Commands in Instructions
medium_findings.description: The skill documents rm -rf and git clean cleanup commands for removing submodules and discarding local changes. These are legitimate Git recovery workflows, but they can delete files if paths are wrong or the command is run in the wrong repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in Markdown examples and directly remove files or untracked content. The context is legitimate submodule maintenance, so this is a usage hazard rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Networked Git Operations May Use Repository Credentials
medium_findings.description: The skill includes clone, fetch, pull, push, and submodule update workflows against remote repositories. These commands are expected for a Git submodule skill, but they can contact untrusted repositories or use local Git credentials if executed automatically.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The networked Git commands are explicit and numerous. No evidence shows credential exfiltration or malicious endpoints, but execution should require user review of remotes.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many backtick locations as Ruby or shell execution. These are Markdown code fences and inline formatting in a documentation-only skill, not executable Ruby code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are fenced Markdown examples in SKILL.md. No Ruby source file or executable script is present in the scanned files.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Examples and References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to example GitHub repositories, a .gitmodules sample, or public documentation references. They do not receive local data in the skill text, but users should replace sample repositories with trusted remotes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in examples and reference lists, not in code that sends data. The residual risk is supply chain exposure if a user copies an example URL without review.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal and Weak Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The path traversal alert maps to cd ../.. in an example workflow, and weak crypto alerts map to Markdown filename or desired-commit wording. No cryptographic operation or arbitrary path access was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is documentation and Git navigation syntax. There is no code path that hashes data or accepts external path input.

low_findings.title: Expected Hidden Git Metadata Access
low_findings.description: The skill instructs users to edit .gitmodules and remove .git/modules cache entries during submodule maintenance. This is expected for Git submodules, but hidden Git metadata changes should be reviewed carefully.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden file references are directly related to Git submodule configuration. They are not secret harvesting, but they affect repository metadata.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands in Markdown Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill is documentation-only, but it contains many shell commands that an agent might execute when helping a user. Review commands before execution, especially commands that alter repositories, push changes, or delete files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly present in the skill text. They are normal Git commands, but their effects depend on the working directory and remote repository.

dangerous_patterns.title: No Prompt Injection Detected
dangerous_patterns.description: No text was found that attempts to override system instructions, skip audit review, claim pre-approval, or impersonate privileged instructions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed skill content is conventional documentation. The confidence is not absolute because the audit is limited to the two scanned files.

content.value_statement: Git submodules are powerful but easy to misconfigure during clone, update, and removal workflows. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured Git submodule guidance with clear operational steps.
actual_capabilities: Explains when Git submodules are useful for shared code and external dependencies.
Provides workflows for adding, cloning, initializing, updating, and removing submodules.
Covers nested submodules, branch tracking, shallow clones, and submodule URL changes.
Gives CI examples for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Jenkins submodule checkout behavior.
Helps troubleshoot detached HEAD states, permission errors, conflicts, and dirty submodules.
limitations: Does not verify that a remote repository URL is trusted or still available.
Does not replace Git expertise for complex merge conflicts or repository recovery.
Contains cleanup commands that can delete files if copied without review.
Does not provide automated scripts or a tested command runner.

use_cases.title: Add a Shared Library
use_cases.description: Plan how to add an external repository as a pinned dependency and commit the submodule reference.

use_cases.title: Repair a Broken Clone
use_cases.description: Initialize missing submodules, inspect status, and restore expected references after a fresh checkout.

use_cases.title: Prepare CI Checkout Rules
use_cases.description: Configure continuous integration jobs so builds include nested submodules consistently.

prompt_templates.title: Understand My Submodules
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner orientation before changing a repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how Git submodules work in this repository. Focus on .gitmodules, pinned commits, and what I should inspect before making changes.

prompt_templates.title: Add a New Submodule
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me add a Git submodule for this trusted repository URL at this path. Show the checks I should run before committing the submodule reference.

prompt_templates.title: Update Existing Submodules
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current submodule status and propose a safe update plan. Include how to inspect diffs before committing the new references.

prompt_templates.title: Recover or Remove a Submodule
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me diagnose this submodule problem and create a recovery plan. Ask before suggesting any command that deletes files or changes Git metadata.

output_examples.input: I need to add a shared UI library as a submodule.
output: A short checklist to confirm the remote URL and target path.
A staged workflow to add the submodule, inspect .gitmodules, and commit the reference.
A reminder to pin the intended branch, tag, or commit before sharing the change.

output_examples.input: My cloned repository is missing submodule contents.
output: A diagnosis path that checks initialization state and recursive submodule status.
A recovery sequence for fetching the referenced commits.
Notes about private repository permissions and SSH access.

output_examples.input: I want to remove a submodule from a project.
output: A removal plan that separates deinitialization, Git index removal, metadata cleanup, and commit review.
Warnings about hidden Git metadata and file deletion commands.
A verification checklist before pushing the cleanup commit.

best_practices: Confirm remote URLs, paths, and repository trust before running clone, update, or push commands.
Inspect submodule status and diffs before committing changes in the parent repository.
Ask for explicit approval before running cleanup commands that delete files or Git metadata.
anti_patterns: Do not copy sample GitHub URLs into production repositories without review.
Do not run recursive update or foreach commands without understanding every submodule target.
Do not use file deletion commands until the current working directory and backup state are confirmed.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Git commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is documentation for Git submodule workflows, but an AI agent may suggest commands based on it.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: It can be used with private repositories, but users should review remote URLs and avoid exposing credentials in prompts.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill contains legitimate Git commands that can fetch network content, push changes, and delete local files.

faq.question: Can it help with nested submodules?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes recursive initialization and update workflows for nested submodule trees.

faq.question: Does it cover CI systems?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Jenkins checkout behavior.

faq.question: What should users verify before cleanup?
faq.answer: Users should verify the working directory, backup state, target path, and pending changes before deleting files or metadata.
